heroOverview: Lymphangitis is an acute inflammation of the lymphatic channels, most recognisably visible as one or more red streaks extending from a wound or skin infection toward the nearest lymph node group. The condition arises when bacteria — most commonly group A Streptococcus or Staphylococcus aureus — invade lymphatic vessels from an adjacent skin infection, wound, or abscess and trigger a rapid inflammatory response within the lymphatic wall. The classic presentation is a tender, warm red streak moving proximally from an infected area on the arm or leg, accompanied by regional lymph node tenderness and systemic signs of infection including fever, chills, and malaise. Untreated lymphangitis can progress within hours to sepsis, making early recognition and prompt antibiotic treatment essential. Most cases respond rapidly to oral antibiotics, but any delay or inadequate treatment raises the risk of bacteraemia, cellulitis extension, and abscess formation.

keyStats.prevalence: Precise global incidence is not established; lymphangitis complicates approximately 2-5% of cellulitis cases severe enough to require hospitalisation
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; most common in otherwise healthy adults with traumatic wounds or skin infections; immunocompromised individuals face higher severity
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in adults aged 20-50 who sustain injuries or have active skin infections; children also affected, particularly with hand and finger infections
keyStats.globalCases: Estimates are not available as a standalone global count; closely tracked as a complication of soft-tissue infection

definition: Lymphangitis (ICD-10: I89.1) is inflammation of the lymphatic channels, characterised pathologically by oedema, lymphocyte and neutrophil infiltration of the lymphatic vessel wall, and often intraluminal bacterial proliferation. The vast majority of cases are acute bacterial lymphangitis, representing spread of infection from a primary focus — most often a skin laceration, puncture wound, insect bite, or cutaneous infection such as cellulitis or an abscess — into the draining lymphatic vessels. The responsible organisms are almost exclusively gram-positive cocci: group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes) in most cases, followed by Staphylococcus aureus, including methicillin-resistant strains (MRSA). The clinical hallmark is the proximal red streak that forms as the bacteria and inflammatory mediators track along the superficial lymphatics toward regional nodes; the streak is tender to palpation and advances at variable speed — sometimes several centimetres per hour. Lymphangitis is classified as acute (most cases), subacute (as in cat-scratch disease from Bartonella henselae), or chronic/recurrent (as in filarial lymphangitis from Wuchereria bancrofti in tropical regions). It falls under general infectious disease management but is classified under cardiology in this institutional coding. Regional lymphadenitis — swollen, tender nodes — accompanies virtually all cases and may progress to suppurative (pus-filled) adenitis requiring drainage.

typesClassification.description: The most common form: rapid-onset red streaking from a wound or skin infection, driven by Streptococcus pyogenes or Staphylococcus aureus; requires urgent antibiotic treatment.

Caused by organisms such as Bartonella henselae (cat-scratch disease), Sporothrix schenckii (sporotrichosis), atypical mycobacteria, or Francisella tularensis; presents with a more indolent nodular lymphocutaneous pattern over days to weeks.
Caused by the filarial nematode Wuchereria bancrofti transmitted by mosquitoes in tropical regions; repeated episodes cause progressive lymphatic damage and eventual lymphoedema (elephantiasis).

Irritant or cytotoxic substances injected or infused near lymphatic vessels can provoke a non-infectious inflammatory lymphangitis; occurs rarely as a complication of intravenous infusions or cutaneous drug reactions.

primarySymptoms: A red streak or streaks extending proximally from a wound, bite, or area of skin infection toward the draining lymph node group — the visually distinctive hallmark of lymphangitis.
Tenderness along the entire length of the red streak, which is warm and slightly raised compared to surrounding skin.
Swollen, tender lymph nodes in the corresponding drainage territory — axillary nodes for arm infections, inguinal nodes for leg infections — with potential fluctuance if suppuration has occurred.
Fever, typically 38-40°C, with chills or rigors reflecting the systemic inflammatory response to bacteraemia or bacterial toxin release.
Generalised malaise, fatigue, and headache accompanying the systemic infectious response.
Localised pain and swelling at the original wound or infection site from which lymphangitis is extending.
Rapid progression — the red streak can visibly extend over several hours, which is a clinical warning sign of advancing infection.
Nausea or loss of appetite in cases with systemic toxicity.
Tachycardia and sometimes hypotension in severe or rapidly progressing cases indicating early sepsis.
In filarial lymphangitis, acute dermatolymphangioadenitis (ADLA) episodes with recurrent limb swelling, redness, and fever during adult filarial death.
earlyWarningSigns: A thin red line appearing from a wound or insect bite and moving up the arm or leg.
A wound that becomes increasingly red and hot with expanding redness beyond its margins.
New tenderness in the armpit or groin associated with a hand or foot infection.
Mild fever and feeling unwell in a person with any skin wound or infection.
emergencySigns: Rapidly advancing red streak visible to be extending over minutes to hours.
High fever above 39°C with shaking chills and confusion — signs of sepsis requiring emergency hospital admission.
Hypotension or altered consciousness in a person with a skin infection — indicates systemic sepsis.
Fluctuant, extremely tender lymph nodes suggesting suppurative adenitis that may require surgical drainage.
Red streaking in an immunocompromised patient (diabetes, HIV, chemotherapy) — always warrants emergency assessment.

causes.description: The most common causative organism; produces streptolysin, streptokinase, and hyaluronidase that allow rapid tissue invasion and spread through lymphatic channels; responsible for the classical red-streaking presentation and associated with potential systemic sepsis within hours of onset.

The second most common cause, particularly in wound-related infections, post-surgical cases, and healthcare-associated lymphangitis; MRSA strains are particularly important in communities with high methicillin-resistant prevalence and in patients with prior antibiotic exposure or healthcare contact.

causes.cause: Puncture wounds, bites, and skin breaks
causes.description: Any disruption of the skin barrier provides an entry point for bacteria colonising the skin surface; animal bites (cats in particular), insect stings, IV drug injection sites, and minor cuts are among the most common inciting events.

Transmitted by cat scratches or bites, this gram-negative organism causes subacute lymphocutaneous lymphangitis with a papule at the inoculation site followed 1-3 weeks later by regional lymphadenopathy; typically self-limiting in immunocompetent individuals.

A dimorphic fungus acquired from rose thorns, sphagnum moss, or other plant material; produces the classic lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis pattern with a primary lesion at the inoculation site and a chain of nodules tracking up the lymphatic distribution — an important differential from bacterial lymphangitis.

causes.cause: Wuchereria bancrofti and lymphatic filariae (tropical lymphangitis)
causes.description: Filarial nematodes cause lymphangitis in the context of lymphatic filariasis, prevalent in tropical sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Pacific islands; repeated inflammatory episodes from adult worm death progressively damage lymphatics, leading to lymphoedema.

riskFactors.description: Any skin disruption — traumatic, surgical, or from chronic skin conditions such as eczema or tinea pedis — provides bacterial entry; proper wound care reduces this risk substantially.

Impaired neutrophil function and microvascular disease increase susceptibility to severe skin and lymphatic infections; diabetics with limb infections have higher rates of hospitalisation and complications from lymphangitis.
HIV infection, haematological malignancies, solid organ transplantation, and cytotoxic chemotherapy all reduce the host's ability to contain a skin infection before it invades lymphatics.
Pre-existing lymphoedema from any cause — post-mastectomy, filarial, or primary — disrupts normal lymphatic flow and local immune surveillance, making recurrent lymphangitis episodes far more likely.
Adipose tissue has reduced perfusion and a pro-inflammatory microenvironment; leg skin infections are more common and more severe, and lymphangitis from lower-limb cellulitis is more frequent.

Non-sterile injections introduce skin flora directly into subcutaneous tissue and adjacent lymphatics; MRSA and unusual organisms such as Clostridium species are encountered, making infections more complex to treat.

riskFactors.factor: Tinea pedis (athlete's foot) and interdigital maceration
riskFactors.description: Fungal toe-web infections create skin fissures that are the most common portal of entry for leg cellulitis and lymphangitis; treating tinea pedis significantly reduces recurrence rates of leg infection.

Living in or travelling to filarial-endemic regions without appropriate mosquito protection and preventive treatment increases exposure to Wuchereria bancrofti, the cause of chronic tropical lymphangitis.

affectedDemographics: Otherwise healthy adults with traumatic wounds or skin infections
People with diabetes or peripheral vascular disease who sustain lower-limb injuries
Immunocompromised individuals including cancer patients on chemotherapy, transplant recipients, and people with HIV
Post-mastectomy patients with ipsilateral arm lymphoedema
Individuals in tropical and subtropical regions exposed to filarial mosquitoes
Children with hand infections from puncture wounds or bites
diagnosisOverview: Lymphangitis is primarily a clinical diagnosis. The combination of a proximal red streak from an infected wound with regional lymph node tenderness, fever, and systemic symptoms is recognised immediately at bedside. Laboratory and microbiological testing support risk stratification and guide antibiotic choice. A full blood count typically shows a raised white cell count with neutrophilia; C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate confirm the inflammatory response; procalcitonin above 0.5 ng/mL suggests bacteraemia. Blood cultures should be obtained in all patients with fever, as bacteraemia is present in 2-4% of cellulitis-associated lymphangitis cases. Wound swabs are taken if there is discharge at the entry wound; swabbing intact skin overlying cellulitis has poor specificity and is not recommended. Ultrasound of the regional lymph nodes helps identify suppurative adenitis (fluctuant, hypoechoic node centres) requiring drainage. In cases with an atypical or indolent course suggesting fungal or mycobacterial infection, the inoculation site and any nodules should be biopsied for tissue culture. The differential diagnosis includes deep vein thrombosis — which also causes a red, swollen, tender limb — but DVT does not produce fever or a proximal streak and is confirmed or excluded by compression duplex ultrasound. Superficial thrombophlebitis also produces a linear redness along a vessel but follows a venous distribution and is not associated with a wound entry site. Filarial lymphangitis is differentiated by geography, serological filarial antigen testing, and nocturnal blood thick film for microfilariae.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count with differential and CRP
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms systemic infection and quantifies inflammatory severity; neutrophilia and elevated CRP support bacterial lymphangitis and help assess the need for hospitalisation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw; results typically available within 2-3 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bacteraemia in cases with high fever or systemic toxicity; identifies the causative organism and antibiotic sensitivities, guiding targeted therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets of blood drawn into sterile bottles from separate venous sites; results take 24-72 hours; negative cultures at 5 days exclude bacteraemia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and its antibiotic susceptibility profile, particularly important for MRSA screening or when initial therapy fails.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile cotton or flocked swab is rotated over the open wound or discharging area; results available in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies suppurative (pus-filled) adenitis requiring surgical or needle drainage, which antibiotics alone cannot treat.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-invasive scan of the affected lymph node group (armpit or groin) using gel and a probe; takes 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin or lymph node biopsy (atypical cases)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Required when fungal infection (sporotrichosis), atypical mycobacteria, or cat-scratch disease is suspected; tissue is submitted for histology and special cultures.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small punch or excisional biopsy under local anaesthetic; the sample is sent to microbiology and pathology; results take 1-4 weeks for fungal and mycobacterial cultures.

diagnosticTests.test: Filarial antigen test and blood film (tropical cases)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects circulating filarial antigen (Wuchereria bancrofti) and identifies microfilariae in nocturnal blood smears to confirm lymphatic filariasis as the cause of chronic recurrent lymphangitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample drawn at night (when microfilaraemia peaks) and sent for thick film examination and immunochromatographic antigen testing.

treatmentOverview: The cornerstone of treatment is prompt systemic antibiotic therapy directed at streptococcal and staphylococcal organisms, which account for the vast majority of acute bacterial lymphangitis. For mild to moderate cases without signs of sepsis in immunocompetent adults, oral antibiotics achieve excellent results: cefalexin (a first-generation cephalosporin) or amoxicillin-clavulanate for 5-10 days is standard first-line; clarithromycin or clindamycin are used in penicillin allergy. When MRSA is suspected — based on prior colonisation, IV drug use, or community MRSA prevalence — oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or doxycycline are added empirically; confirmed MRSA requires vancomycin or linezolid. Patients with fever, rapid spread, lymphangitis in immunocompromised hosts, or failure of initial oral therapy are hospitalised for intravenous antibiotics — typically IV flucloxacillin or cefazolin for methicillin-susceptible organisms; vancomycin for MRSA or penicillin-allergic patients. The underlying wound or primary focus must be treated concurrently: adequate wound cleaning, debridement of necrotic tissue, and drainage of any abscess are essential to source control; antibiotics alone cannot clear an infection where there is undrained pus. Limb elevation reduces oedema and lymphatic congestion. In most patients, fever begins to resolve within 24-48 hours of effective antibiotics and the red streak stops advancing within 12-24 hours; complete resolution of streaking takes 3-7 days. Suppurative lymphadenitis may require needle aspiration or surgical incision and drainage in addition to antibiotics. Filarial lymphangitis is treated with diethylcarbamazine (DEC) combined with albendazole.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral cefalexin or amoxicillin-clavulanate (first-line for mild-moderate)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic for community-acquired bacterial lymphangitis from streptococcal or staphylococcal organisms; covers the most common causative bacteria with good oral bioavailability; a 5-10 day course is standard.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates of 85-92% for uncomplicated bacterial lymphangitis and cellulitis in outpatient studies; response evident within 48-72 hours in most cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous flucloxacillin or cefazolin (hospital admission)
medicalTreatments.description: Used when oral therapy is inappropriate: high fever, rapid progression, systemic toxicity, immunosuppression, or treatment failure; covers streptococcal and methicillin-sensitive staphylococcal organisms effectively.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of fever within 24-48 hours and arrest of streaking in 70-85% of hospitalised patients within 48 hours; duration typically 5-7 days IV, followed by oral step-down.

medicalTreatments.name: Vancomycin (for confirmed or suspected MRSA)
medicalTreatments.description: The standard intravenous agent for MRSA lymphangitis; dosed according to weight and renal function with trough monitoring; oral linezolid or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole are alternatives for non-severe MRSA.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bacteriological cure in approximately 70-80% of MRSA skin and soft-tissue infections; clinical success rate 75-90% in combination with source control (drainage).

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole (for sporotrichosis lymphangitis)
medicalTreatments.description: The drug of choice for Sporothrix schenckii lymphocutaneous infection; oral itraconazole 100-200 mg daily for 3-6 months is standard; saturated solution of potassium iodide (SSKI) is a historical alternative in resource-limited settings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 90-95% in lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis with full itraconazole course; relapse is uncommon with adequate duration.

medicalTreatments.name: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) plus albendazole (for filarial lymphangitis)
medicalTreatments.description: The standard treatment for lymphatic filariasis; DEC is directly filaricidal and also reduces microfilaraemia; albendazole adds synergistic macrofilaricidal activity; used in mass drug administration programmes and individually.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: A single co-administered dose reduces microfilaraemia by approximately 99% at 12 months; multiple annual doses are needed to achieve sustained suppression and prevent lymphoedema progression.

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage of suppurative lymphadenitis
surgicalOptions.description: When lymph nodes become fluctuant and pus-filled despite antibiotics, surgical incision and drainage removes the loculated collection; a drain may be left in place for 24-48 hours.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of suppurative lymphadenitis in approximately 90-95% after drainage combined with antibiotic therapy; recurrence is uncommon with adequate source control.

surgicalOptions.name: Wound debridement and washout
surgicalOptions.description: For necrotising extension or retained foreign bodies at the entry wound, surgical debridement removes necrotic tissue that prevents antibiotic penetration and supports bacterial survival.
surgicalOptions.successRate: When performed promptly, surgical debridement prevents progression to necrotising fasciitis in the majority of at-risk cases; outcomes depend on timing and extent of tissue involvement.

alternativeTreatments.name: Limb elevation
alternativeTreatments.description: Elevating the affected arm or leg above heart level reduces hydrostatic pressure in lymphatics, decreases oedema, and improves lymphatic flow; improves comfort and may reduce the rate of proximal spread during early treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Warm compresses to the affected area
alternativeTreatments.description: Applied to the entry wound (not the lymphatic streak itself), warm compresses improve local blood flow and may facilitate immune cell recruitment; they should not replace antibiotics and are a comfort measure.

alternativeTreatments.name: Management of underlying lymphoedema
alternativeTreatments.description: In patients with recurrent lymphangitis from pre-existing lymphoedema, prophylactic use of compression garments, meticulous skin hygiene, and prompt treatment of tinea pedis or other skin breaks reduces recurrence frequency.

whySeeSpecialist: Most straightforward acute bacterial lymphangitis is managed by a general practitioner or emergency medicine physician. An infectious disease specialist should be involved for: suspected MRSA or unusual organisms, immunocompromised hosts, failure of initial antibiotic therapy within 48-72 hours, recurrent episodes, or lymphangitis in a geographic context suggesting filariasis or sporotrichosis. A surgeon is needed when suppurative lymphadenitis or abscess formation requires drainage. Lymphoedema specialist referral helps patients with recurrent lymphangitis secondary to chronic lymphatic dysfunction.
doctorSelectionGuide: Acute lymphangitis presenting to an emergency department or urgent care should be assessed immediately; this is not a condition to manage expectantly over several days. For recurrent infections, a dermatologist or infectious disease physician can identify predisposing factors such as tinea pedis, chronic skin conditions, or underlying lymphoedema. For tropical lymphangitis, a travel medicine or tropical disease specialist provides access to filarial diagnostic testing and treatment protocols.
preventionStrategies: Clean all wounds promptly with soap and water; apply topical antiseptic and cover with a clean dressing.
Treat tinea pedis (athlete's foot) aggressively — it is the most preventable portal of entry for leg lymphangitis.
Wear protective footwear in environments with risk of puncture injuries; use gloves when handling thorny plants.
Control blood glucose in diabetes to reduce susceptibility to severe skin infection.
In patients with lymphoedema, use compression garments, maintain meticulous skin hygiene, and seek early antibiotics for any skin break.
Travellers to filarial-endemic regions should use DEET-containing repellents and wear long clothing to prevent mosquito bites.
Seek rapid medical attention for any wound that becomes increasingly red, warm, or swollen — do not wait for a streak to appear.
lifestyleModifications: Elevate the affected limb above heart level as much as possible during treatment to reduce swelling.
Complete the full antibiotic course even if symptoms improve after 24-48 hours; partial courses select for resistant organisms.
Do not attempt to squeeze or lance lymph nodes at home; suppurative nodes require sterile drainage.
For patients with recurrent leg lymphangitis: lose excess weight if obese, treat skin conditions, and use compression stocking after resolution.
Return immediately if fever returns after initial improvement or if the red streak resumes advancing.

recommended: Adequate protein intake to support wound healing and immune function
High fluid intake to maintain hydration during febrile illness
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, berries, capsicum) to support collagen synthesis in healing tissues
avoid: Alcohol during active infection, as it impairs immune function and interacts with some antibiotics
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs as the sole management strategy — they may reduce fever but do not treat the infection and can mask progression

exerciseGuidelines: Rest the affected limb and avoid vigorous exercise until fever resolves and the red streak has stopped advancing — typically 24-48 hours into effective antibiotic treatment. Walking is acceptable for lower-limb lymphangitis once systemic symptoms have settled. Return to full activity should follow complete resolution of the red streak and lymph node tenderness, usually within 5-10 days of treatment. For post-mastectomy patients: contact your lymphoedema physiotherapist, as infection can trigger or worsen lymphoedema and specific arm exercises and sleeve use should be reviewed.
dailyManagement: Mark the advancing edge of the red streak with a ballpoint pen at the time of starting antibiotics; if the line continues to advance beyond the mark 12-24 hours later, return to emergency care.
Take antibiotics at regular intervals as prescribed; do not halve doses or skip doses because you feel better.
Monitor wound temperature and swelling at the entry site daily.
Watch lymph node sites for increasing tenderness, hardness, or fluctuance — report these promptly.
For recurrent lymphangitis patients: have a written action plan from your physician to start antibiotics within 24 hours of a recognisable episode.
prognosis: Acute bacterial lymphangitis treated promptly with appropriate antibiotics carries an excellent prognosis. Fever typically resolves within 24-48 hours, the streak stops advancing within 12-24 hours, and full recovery is expected within 1-2 weeks. Mortality from uncomplicated lymphangitis is rare in immunocompetent adults in countries with access to antibiotics. However, delayed treatment raises the risk of bacteraemia (occurring in 2-4% of cellulitis-associated lymphangitis cases), necrotising fasciitis, septic shock, and death — outcomes that are entirely preventable with early intervention. Patients who develop recurrent lymphangitis, particularly those with underlying lymphoedema, face a pattern of repeated acute episodes that progressively damage lymphatic architecture and worsen oedema. Chronic tropical lymphangitis from filariasis carries significant long-term morbidity from lymphoedema progression.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients experience fever resolution within 24-48 hours of starting appropriate antibiotics. The red streak stops advancing within 12-24 hours; existing inflammation and redness fade over 3-7 days. Regional lymph node tenderness may persist for 1-2 weeks after the streak resolves. A full course of antibiotics (typically 5-10 days) is required, and activity can resume once systemic symptoms settle. Suppurative lymphadenitis after drainage takes 1-3 weeks to fully heal, with possible residual scar tissue in the node.
complications: Bacteraemia and sepsis — bacteria enter the bloodstream; early signs include very high fever, rigors, rapid pulse, and confusion, requiring emergency intravenous antibiotics and supportive care.
Suppurative lymphadenitis — lymph nodes become pus-filled and require drainage; may lead to chronic scarring or a draining sinus if not treated adequately.
Necrotising fasciitis — rare progression to deep-plane necrotising infection, a surgical emergency with significant mortality if not recognised and debrided rapidly.
Recurrent lymphangitis and progressive lymphoedema — repeated episodes damage lymphatic vessel architecture, reducing drainage capacity and leading to permanent limb swelling.
Antibiotic resistance — inadequate or incomplete treatment selects for resistant organisms including MRSA, complicating future episodes.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cellulitis
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient information on cellulitis, the most common infection predisposing to lymphangitis, including symptoms, treatment and when to seek emergency care.

supportResources.name: CDC — Soft Tissue Infections
supportResources.description: CDC information on group A streptococcal infections, the most common cause of acute bacterial lymphangitis, including prevention and treatment guidance.

supportResources.name: WHO — Lymphatic Filariasis
supportResources.description: WHO fact sheet on lymphatic filariasis, the cause of chronic tropical lymphangitis and lymphoedema, covering global burden, treatment, and elimination programmes.

sources.title: Stevens DL et al. Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections: 2014 Update by the IDSA
Bisno AL, Stevens DL. Streptococcal infections of skin and soft tissues. NEJM 1996
NICE NG141 — Cellulitis and erysipelas: antimicrobial prescribing. NICE 2019
Kauffman CA. Sporotrichosis. Clin Infect Dis 1999
Raff AB, Kroshinsky D. Cellulitis: A Review. JAMA 2016

confusedWithConditions.name: Erysipelas
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Erysipelas is an acute dermal infection with a sharply demarcated raised edge and salmon-red colour, caused almost exclusively by group A Streptococcus; lymphangitis shows a streaking red line from a wound tracking toward lymph nodes and may coexist with erysipelas.

confusedWithConditions.name: Filariasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Filarial lymphangitis is a chronic tropical form caused by nematode parasites rather than bacteria; it presents with recurrent episodes of limb swelling, redness, and fever without a visible entry wound or proximal streak, and is diagnosed with serological antigen testing rather than wound cultures.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lymphoma causes painless lymph node enlargement that develops over weeks to months without fever or erythema; lymphangitis causes acutely tender, warm lymph nodes alongside a red streak and fever from infection.

relatedConditions.name: Erysipelas
relatedConditions.relevance: Erysipelas is a closely related superficial streptococcal skin infection that frequently coexists with or precipitates lymphangitis; both affect the skin and lymphatics and are treated with the same antibiotic agents.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphatic filariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The chronic tropical cause of lymphangitis from Wuchereria bancrofti nematodes; repeated filarial lymphangitis episodes cause the progressive lymphoedema characteristic of advanced filariasis.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphoedema rehabilitation
relatedConditions.relevance: Pre-existing lymphoedema is both a cause of recurrent lymphangitis and a consequence of repeated episodes; lymphoedema management is integral to preventing further attacks.

faqs.question: What does lymphangitis look like?
faqs.answer: The most visible sign is a thin red streak or streaks extending from an infected wound, bite, or skin sore toward the nearest lymph node group — up the forearm toward the armpit, or up the lower leg toward the groin. The streak is warm and tender. Nearby lymph nodes are also swollen and sore.

faqs.question: Is lymphangitis serious?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it requires prompt treatment. Without antibiotics, the bacteria spreading through the lymphatics can enter the bloodstream within hours, causing sepsis. When treated early with appropriate antibiotics, most cases resolve completely within 1-2 weeks without complications.

faqs.question: What causes lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: Bacterial lymphangitis is almost always caused by Streptococcus pyogenes or Staphylococcus aureus spreading from a wound, cut, insect bite, or skin infection into the lymphatic channels. MRSA is an increasingly common cause in community settings.

faqs.question: How is lymphangitis treated?
faqs.answer: The main treatment is antibiotics — usually oral cefalexin or amoxicillin-clavulanate for mild cases, or intravenous antibiotics in hospital for severe cases. The underlying wound must also be cleaned and any abscess drained. Most people improve visibly within 24-48 hours of starting the right antibiotic.

faqs.question: Can lymphangitis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: No. Bacterial lymphangitis does not resolve without antibiotics and can progress rapidly to sepsis. Any red streak extending from a wound with fever requires same-day medical assessment and antibiotic treatment. Do not wait to see if it improves without treatment.

faqs.question: Is the red streak on my arm dangerous?
faqs.answer: A red streak extending from a wound toward your armpit or groin is the classic sign of lymphangitis and requires immediate medical attention — the same day. It means bacteria are spreading through your lymphatic system. Early antibiotic treatment prevents progression to sepsis.

faqs.question: What antibiotics treat lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: For most cases, oral cefalexin or amoxicillin-clavulanate for 5-10 days is first-line. If MRSA is suspected, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or doxycycline is added. Severe cases require intravenous antibiotics in hospital — flucloxacillin, cefazolin, or vancomycin depending on the organism.

faqs.question: What is the difference between lymphangitis and cellulitis?
faqs.answer: Cellulitis is a broader bacterial infection of the skin and subcutaneous tissue, presenting as a diffuse red, warm, swollen area. Lymphangitis specifically involves the lymphatic channels and produces the characteristic proximal red streak. The two often occur together, with lymphangitis developing as a complication of cellulitis.

faqs.question: How long does lymphangitis last?
faqs.answer: With appropriate antibiotics, fever usually resolves within 24-48 hours and the red streak stops advancing within 12-24 hours. Full resolution of redness and lymph node swelling takes 5-14 days. The antibiotic course should be completed in full even when symptoms improve quickly.

faqs.question: Can lymphangitis spread to the rest of the body?
faqs.answer: Yes — this is the key risk. Without adequate treatment, bacteria in the lymphatic channels can enter the bloodstream and cause bacteraemia and sepsis, which is life-threatening. That is why a red streak extending from an infection is treated as a medical urgency.

faqs.question: What is a red streak from a wound?
faqs.answer: A red streak extending from a wound is the visible sign of lymphangitis — bacteria from the wound spreading along the lymphatic vessels toward the nearest lymph node. It is a medical emergency requiring same-day antibiotic treatment to prevent progression to bloodstream infection.

faqs.question: Who is at most risk of lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: People with open wounds, skin infections, diabetes, immunosuppression from medications or illness, existing lymphoedema, or intravenous drug use face the highest risk. Tinea pedis (athlete's foot) is the most common preventable entry point for leg lymphangitis in otherwise healthy adults.

faqs.question: Can I treat lymphangitis at home?
faqs.answer: Only the mildest cases — a small streak in an otherwise healthy person without fever, starting within hours — may be managed at home with oral antibiotics prescribed by a doctor. Any fever, rapid progression, lymph node swelling, or symptoms in an immunocompromised person require hospital assessment. Never treat a red streak with home remedies alone.

faqs.question: Is lymphangitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Lymphangitis itself is not contagious — it is not spread from person to person. However, the bacteria causing it (Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, MRSA) can be transmitted through close skin contact or shared items. Standard hand hygiene and wound covering prevent bacterial spread.

faqs.question: Can lymphangitis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly in patients with underlying lymphoedema, diabetes, obesity, or chronic skin conditions. Each episode further damages lymphatic channels, increasing the risk of future attacks. Identifying and managing the predisposing factor — such as treating tinea pedis or wearing compression garments — is essential to preventing recurrence.

faqs.question: What happens if lymphangitis is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated lymphangitis can progress within hours to days to bacteraemia, sepsis, necrotising fasciitis, or organ failure. It is not a condition that resolves on its own. The risk of these severe complications is why same-day medical assessment and antibiotics are always required when a red streak is identified.

faqs.question: Does lymphangitis cause permanent damage?
faqs.answer: A single treated episode rarely causes permanent damage. Repeated episodes damage lymphatic vessels, gradually reducing drainage capacity and potentially causing chronic lymphoedema in the affected limb. Suppurative lymphadenitis can leave scarring in affected lymph nodes. Preventing recurrence is therefore important.

faqs.question: Can animal bites cause lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cat bites and scratches are a particularly common cause — both from direct bacterial inoculation of skin flora and from Bartonella henselae, which causes cat-scratch disease with a distinct subacute lymphangitis pattern. Any animal bite wound that becomes red, warm, or streaky requires antibiotic treatment.

faqs.question: What is filarial lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: Filarial lymphangitis is a chronic form seen in tropical regions caused by Wuchereria bancrofti nematodes transmitted by mosquitoes. Episodes of acute limb swelling, redness, and fever recur over years and progressively damage the lymphatic system, eventually causing permanent lymphoedema. Treatment involves diethylcarbamazine and albendazole.

metaDescription: Lymphangitis causes a red streak spreading from a wound toward lymph nodes with fever. Learn symptoms, antibiotic treatment, and when it is an emergency.
